coredata.dc:title: Nanotechnology-based analytical techniques for the detection of contaminants in aquatic products
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chengke
Sun, Shuyang
Wang, Ping
Zhao, Huawei
Li, Wenling
coredata.dc:description: Food safety of aquatic products has attracted considerable attention worldwide. Although a series of conventional bioassays and instrumental methods have been developed for the detection of pathogenic bacteria, heavy metal residues, marine toxins, and biogenic amines during the production and storage of fish, shrimp, crabs et al., the nanotechnology-based analyses still have their advantages and are promising since they are cost-efficient, highly sensitive and selective, easy to conduct, facial design, often require no sophisticated instruments but with excellent detection performance. This review aims to summarize the advances of various biosensing strategies for bacteria, metal ions, and small molecule contaminants in aquatic products during the last five years, The review highlights the development in nanotechnologies applied for biorecognition process, signal transduction and amplification methods in each novel approach, the nuclease-mediated DNA amplification, nanomaterials (noble metal nanoparticle, metal-organic frameworks, carbon dots), lateral flow-based biosensor, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, microfluidic chip, and molecular imprinting technologies were especially emphasized. Moreover, this study provides a view of current accomplishments, challenges, and future development directions of nanotechnology in aquatic product safety evaluation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Food safety of aquatic products has attracted considerable attention worldwide. Although a series of conventional bioassays and instrumental methods have been developed for the detection of pathogenic bacteria, heavy metal residues, marine toxins, and biogenic amines during the production and storage of fish, shrimp, crabs et al., the nanotechnology-based analyses still have their advantages and are promising since they are cost-efficient, highly sensitive and selective, easy to conduct, facial design, often require no sophisticated instruments but with excellent detection performance. This review aims to summarize the advances of various biosensing strategies for bacteria, metal ions, and small molecule contaminants in aquatic products during the last five years, The review highlights the development in nanotechnologies applied for biorecognition process, signal transduction and amplification methods in each novel approach, the nuclease-mediated DNA amplification, nanomaterials (noble metal nanoparticle, metal-organic frameworks, carbon dots), lateral flow-based biosensor, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, microfluidic chip, and molecular imprinting technologies were especially emphasized. Moreover, this study provides a view of current accomplishments, challenges, and future development directions of nanotechnology in aquatic product safety evaluation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aquatic products, like fish, shrimp, shellfish, crab et al. provide high-quality proteins and peptides, unsaturated fatty acids, vitamins, and many other nutrients that are less commonly found in other foods, thereby playing a crucial role in human's daily life. However, due to the presence of abundant proteins, carbohydrates, and nucleic acids in aquatic products, as well as the water-rich environment that is helpful for the proliferation of pathogenic bacteria, they are more susceptible to a series of harmful pathogenic microorganisms in comparison to the livestock and poultry meat counterpart [1–3]. In the meantime, harmful biogenic amines and bacterial toxins, as well as marine biotoxins also entail highly concerned food safety risks. Furthermore, with the development of modern industry, heavy metal wastes released into lakes, rivers, and oceans are prone to accumulate in aquatic products (e.g., fish and seafood), and lead to serious consequences to human health through the food chain. Therefore, rapid and sensitive analytical methods are meaningful and urgently needed to detect these contaminants in aquatic products.

Recently, numerous methods have been developed for bacteria and small molecular contaminants detection, including traditional microbiological assay, which has a low technical threshold for operation, however, relatively long operating time, tedious operation process, and low detection accuracy hinder its development. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is another sensitive method and has been widely used for bacteria and food contaminants detection, yet it relies on high-affinity antibodies, the activity of antibodies from different batches varies a lot, and there are very few antibodies for many harmful contaminants (e.g., heavy metal ions and lethal compounds). The instrumental detection methods, like gas and high-performance liquid chromatography (GC/HPLC), mass spectrometry (MS) were applied for antibiotic and small molecules detection, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) were applied for heavy metal ions detection. There is no doubt that these methods have their advantages, like high-throughput capability and great precision of detection. However, the need for expensive instruments and well-trained technicians leads to lengthy and costly processes, which are not suitable for on-site detection. On the other hand, the increasingly restricted food safety laws and regulations, and fast in-situ analysis with more sensitivity, selectivity, and accuracy have attracted more and more attention. Furthermore, at present, the delicate equipment and professionals are usually scant, trace amounts but wide varieties of harmful contaminants are usually found in aquatic products. Therefore, novel detection strategies capable of rapid, sensitive, real-time, and on-site monitoring of contaminants are meaningful and in great demand in the aquacultural field.

In recent years, nanotechnology advancements for contaminants detection in aquatic products have drawn extensive attention due to their multifaceted superiorities, like high sensitivity and selectivity, simple to operate, time and labor-saving, facile and affordable devices [4–6]. Through the signal amplification, typical detection limits by using nanotechnology are at the nM level, or even fM in some cases (the detection sensitivity is enhanced dramatically); several affinity molecules including aptamers, antibodies, and complexing agents were decorated on the nanomaterials to improve the selectivity of detection (the types of recognition molecules are increasing and have higher affinity with targets); the highly active surface of biosensors accelerated the target recognition process and dramatically reduced the assay time to an hour or less, and the usage of reagents to a level of μL to nL (the detection process is easier to operate). Finally, most nanotechnologies work well with colorimetric, fluorescent, and electrochemical detection, providing a variety of robust ways to monitor the food safety of aquatic products by using portable devices (e.g., smart phones) and artificial intelligence-assisted methods (the detection does not rely on large instruments and signal detection methods have more choices). As shown in Chart 1, the annual histogram of the publication number of nanotechnology and aquatic products since 2010 demonstrates substantial research growth from 2010 to 2020, and a rapid increase since 2020, which indicates that nanotechnology applied in the field of aquatic product safety monitoring has attracted increasing attention.

Therefore, it is necessary to summarize the recent progress and future development trends of nanotechnology for aquatic product safety assessment. Few reports have mentioned the concept, signal generation mode, design strategy of biosensors, and their applications for small molecule analysis in the environment, clinic, and food area [7]. However, a comprehensive review of the applications, as well as the advantages and drawbacks of each nanotechnology are rarely mentioned. In this review, we summarize the latest developments in nanotechnology for monitoring different kinds of contaminants, stressing new and interesting biosensing materials, detection strategies, signal acquisition, and amplification protocols (see Scheme 1 ). Herein, the elaborated nanotechnologies are mainly classified according to their varieties, surface modification of nanomaterials, and signal amplification strategies. Furthermore, the applications of these methods are systematically presented based on the types of targets, i.e., pathogenic bacteria, heavy metal ions, biotoxins, and biogenic amines. The superiors and limitations of each technology are discussed from our point of view.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General nanotechnologies for aquatic food safety measurement
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, many nanotechnology strategies have been developed for improving detection efficiency. Based on the sensing platforms involved, they could be classified as nucleic acids-based, nanomaterials-based, lateral flow assay (LFA)-based, molecule imprinted polymers (MIP)-based, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS)-based, and microfluidic devices-based biosensor.

Due to their flexible spatial structures, DNA or RNA were frequently used in the modification of nanomaterials to prepare novel biosensors, especially for analytical performance improvement by signal amplification. The nuclease-mediated DNA amplification approaches are widely used to increase the concentrations of target molecules and improve the detection sensitivity. Nowadays, more and more culture-independent approaches have undergone significant development in microbial ecology [8–10]. For example, foodborne pathogenic bacteria can be quantified by detecting pathogen DNA or RNA, which often has higher sensitivity and simplicity. Various nucleic acid-based biosensors [11], like polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [12] and its deviations, quantitative PCR (q-PCR), reverse transcription PCR (RT-PCR) [13] are faster, more sensitive and selective, and do not need the special enrichment medium [14], due to the amounts of targets (DNA or RNA) can be easily amplified for many orders of magnitude; especially, these nuclease-based DNA amplification methods can detect dead cells or viable but non-cultivable cells, which is beneficial for pathogens or virus detection in aquatic products [15]. There are still some drawbacks of the nucleic acids-based method, e.g., the consumption and types of reagents were increased (often several DNA segments and multiple nucleases were needed); special instruments for nucleic acids extending are often needed, which will increase the cost and time of detection and is not friendly to some laboratories in developing countries and poor areas with limited equipment. These problems can be alleviated by using the isothermal amplification reaction and the development of automated small instruments.

Nanomaterials can serve as effective tools to enhance the detection efficiency since their response to the surrounding environment [16–18]. Three functional nanomaterials, i.e., noble metal nanoparticles, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), and carbon materials were frequently used for harmful contaminants detection in aquatic products [19].

Noble metal nanoparticles, including gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [20,21], silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [22], Au/Pt hybrid nanoparticles [23,24] (see Fig. 1 A and B) have excellent properties, like distinct color changes if monodispersed particles aggregated induced by the metal ions, fluorescence intensity changes due to the inner filter effect or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect after binding with target molecules [25], electrical conductivity changes due to the charge shielding effect after the pathogens attached to the electrode [26,27], therefore are widely used for aquatic product food safety detection [28].

MOFs are a series of crystalline porous materials with well-defined 3D structures that are prepared by using metals linked together by organic ligands as bridging, which presents high porosity, abundant functional groups, and tunable physical and chemical properties [30], and were widely used for matrix separation, contaminant removal, catalysis, biosensing (see Fig. 1C) [31,32]. In addition, MOFs-based sensors have huge capacities for post-synthetic modification, and the capacity to be biofunctionalized with functional molecules, like antibodies or oligonucleotides. Their remarkable catalytical properties, and complex matrix separation capability coupled with strong luminescent characterization make them suitable candidates for food safety evaluation of aquatic products [33].

Carbon-based materials, including carbon dots (CDs), carbon nanotubes (CNT), and graphene have also been increasingly attractive recently (see Fig. 1D) [20]. CDs have the merit of unique optical properties, easy to prepare and surface modification, high compatibility, and low cost [34,35]; while CNT and graphene offer large surface-to-volume ratios, high electric conductivity, and abundant surface functional groups, which render them promising platforms applied in aquatic products quality and safety detection [36].

It must be noted that although nanomaterials have different advantages, like simple preparation and labeling process, excellent photoelectric performance, and low cost (if considering the very little usage in one detection), there are still some areas that need improvement, e.g., the stability of nanomaterials is not satisfactory in a complex environment (like high concentrations of salt and interfering substances in seawater and seafood); the properties of sensors from the different batch are often not the same; many detection methods have good performance only in the artificial buffer solution and in a short period. Therefore, the synthesis techniques and surface modification methods for biosensors should be improved and standardized, the pre-treatment of practical samples by using magnetic nanoparticles will reduce the interference of matrix substances.

Due to its advantages, like user-friendliness, cost-effectiveness, ease of production, and high portability, the LFA is an ideal and broadly used technology and is applied for environmental monitoring, clinical diagnosis, and food safety detection (see Fig. 2 A) [37–39]. In the nanotechnology-based LFA, the recognition elements on the nanomaterials include antibodies, aptamers, lectins, and other small affinity biomolecules, which could recognize the target in complex samples, while the nanomaterials were used to translate the recognition process into optical, magnetic, or electrochemical signals. Meanwhile, nanomaterials are frequently used for signal amplification [40], which significantly enhances the sensitivity and performance of LFA. However, the quantitative analysis ability of LFAs is strongly limited by strip reader devices that can accurately measure the colorimetric, fluorescent or electrical signal; the temperature and humidity of the surrounding environment as well as the reaction time have significant influences on the detection, and are important factors that contribute to the instability of LFA-based assay; the standards and procedures for making test strips are not uniform, which also interfere with the detection capability. This problem can be alleviated by the cooperation of laboratory researchers and industrial manufacturers.

Many other approaches have also been applied to improve the capability of detection methods. For example, to enhance the selectivity of the assay and eliminate the interference of sample matrices, biosensors integrated with MIPs are promising candidates (see Fig. 2B) [44,45]. MIP is physically and chemically stable, easy to prepare, and can be reused for a long period, which significantly reduces the detection cost [41]. We must note that due to the performance of MIP-based biosensors being influenced by the template molecules, the types and derivatization process of model molecules determine the selectivity of detection. Also, cross-reaction is the main problem in the actual application of biosensors, especially considering there are many similar molecules or intermediate products in the metamorphic process of aquatic food, which might hinder their wide applications.

Compared with traditional detection techniques, due to their advantages like being simple, automatic, and using portable miniaturized systems, microfluidic devices have attracted lots of attention, especially for the efficient concentration and detection of pathogens and harmful contaminants in aquatic products (see Fig. 2C) [42,46,47]. Meanwhile, SERS has also attracted increasing interest due to its superior features like simplicity, rapid measurement, and high sensitivity [43,48]. The compact handheld Raman device and non-destructive detection procedure facilitate the in-situ sensing of multicomponent samples and have been demonstrated to be an effective detection strategy for aquatic products (see Fig. 2D) [49]. Although these new-type detection methods have various advantages, there are remaining weaknesses in the assay. The construction of microfluidic devices hindered their wide applications, and decreased their versatility if considering the difference in the target and samples being tested; the SERS-based method needs reliable nanoplatforms, which often rely on the stable modification process of nanomaterials, therefore, the anti-interference ability of the SERS method needs to be improved when implied for aquatic food safety detection.

Detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria has attracted extensive attention [50,51], since they are easily produced, rapidly proliferated, and are widely spread in aquaculture, transportation, and storage of aquatic products [52–54]. Traditional methods including the plate count method suffer from limitations of low sensitivity, complex procedures, and time-consuming operations [55,56]. Emerging analytical methods based on nanotechnology have been widely developed, which make pathogens detected more accurately and easier (see Table 1 ) [54,57,58]. In this section, detection methods are classified according to the species of bacteria, including Vibrio parahaemolyticus, Vibrio cholerae, Vibrio vulnificus, Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella, and Listeria monocytogenes.

Three main Vibrio species, V. parahaemolyticus, V. cholerae, and V. vulnificus are frequently found pathogens that are responsible for a dramatic increase in seafood-related infections worldwide [66,67]. Therefore, early and quick detection of these species is important [68].

Isothermal amplification technology has merits, as it does not require temperature-changing equipment and is more convenient compared with traditional PCR [69,70,71]. As shown in Fig. 3 , a loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) assay was developed to detect the tlh gene in Vibrio parahaemolyticus pathogen from 62 seafood samples including clams and shrimp [72]. Hydroxynaphthol blue (HNB) was used as a readily available fluorescent dye during DNA amplification. this method is more sensitive than conventional PCR, specific, and cost-effective. However, the complexity of the operation, usage of many reagents and several DNA segments made it not convenient in the actual applications.

LFA is also frequently used for pathogen detection due to its advantages like simple, fast, detection results that are easy to observe. Different kinds of gold nanomaterials were frequently used in LFA, Wu et al. [73] compared four types of gold nanoparticles to see their performance on immunochromatographic strips for visual detection of V. parahaemolyticus, as low as 5.5 × 105 CFU/mL can be detected in six types of seafood samples (i.e., white clam, flower clam, razor clam, yellow croaker, and fresh shrimp). The prepared strip is very stable in at least 14 weeks, and the detection results were consistent with PCR but with the advantages of being simple, cost-efficient, and easy to conduct.

To eliminate the interferences of matrix components, magnetic separation-assisted sample pretreatment coupled with the fluorescence method was applied for V. parahaemolyticus detection, as shown in Fig. 4 A, Zhai et al. [74] synthesized IgG conjugated magnetic Fe3O4 NPs and IgY conjugated quantum dots (QDs), these two antibodies can specifically bind with V. parahaemolyticus in a sandwich form, the V. parahaemolyticus were detected by measuring the fluorescence intensity of QDs, the LOD of this method is 100 CFU/mL. The magnetic pretreatment of samples, as well as the QDs-based fluorescence detection enhance the detection sensitivity.

Electrochemical methods mostly rely on the surface modification of electrodes; therefore, nanomaterials are frequently used for the decoration of electrodes and further for pathogen detection in aquatic products. As shown in Fig. 4B, a photoelectrochemical aptamer sensor has been developed to detect V. parahaemolyticus in shellfish (conch, clam, astarte) [75], Bi2WO6 (BWO-1) was firstly decorated onto ITO electrode, then Ag2S was formed in situ on the BWO-1 layer, finally, AuNPs were modified onto the electrode to link thiol functionalized aptamer through Au–S bond. Since the pathogen-aptamer complex has a greater steric hindrance effect and inhibits electron transferring, the photocurrent will decrease significantly. The LOD of V. parahaemolyticus is 40 CFU/mL. This method exhibits good specificity, stability and reproducibility. However, the readout of the electrochemical signal often needs a special instrument and is not as intuitive as the spectroscopic methods, the difference between electrodes may also result in bias of the detection results, which may interfere with the stability of the detection.

V. cholerae is the causative agent of pandemic cholera in humans and is frequently found in aquatic environments [78,79]. It is important to determine if seafood is contaminated with toxigenic V. cholerae, and early diagnosis of suspected cholera cases to prevent the spread of this disease [79,80].

LAMP method was used to quickly and visually detect virulence-related genes, i.e., ace, zot, cri, and nanH, and the infectious process of V. cholerae in fish and shellfish. The one-step LAMP reaction was conducted at 65 °C for 40 min, and the positive results were inspected by a light green color under visible light or green fluorescence under UV light, the LOD of this method ranges from 2.5 to 400 CFU/reaction for different targets from V. cholerae [81]. This method is sensitive, without multiple heating and cooling cycles, but the need for many reagents and relatively complex procedures may bring inconvenience to the test.

LFA was also developed for V. cholerae detection in shrimp, blood clams, mussels, and oysters [76], as shown in Fig. 4C, the samples were preincubated in alkaline peptone for 24 h to improve the sensitivity, two monoclonal antibodies, named VC-63 and VC-201 were used to recognize the pathogen in a sandwich manner at T-line, as low as 1 CFU/mL V. cholerae can be detected, the merit of this strip test include simple, rapid and sensitive due to the dual-recognition of two antibodies.

V. vulnificus is one of the most deadly infectious halophilic species known to cause septicemia [82]. It is critical to intensively control V. vulnificus infection due to the intake and distribution of seafood, especially shrimp and raw oysters [83,84].

To enhance the sensitivity of the detection, pathogen-specific genes were amplified with the assistance of diverse nucleases, as shown in Fig. 4D, a RAA assay and the CRISPR/Cas12a system were used for V. vulniﬁcus detection in shrimp [77]. The vvhA gene in V. vulniﬁcus was amplified with RAA assay and detected by using the fluorescent method, this assay is rapid (less than 40 min) and sensitive with a LOD of the pathogen is 2 copies/reaction. However, the stability of this method should be considered since multiple steps were adopted, and the detection cost also needs to be reduced in the future.

S. aureus is a common foodborne pathogen that can cause a suppurative infection after eating contaminated food. Detection of S. aureus in aquatic products plays an important role in the assurance of food safety [85].

The antibiotics were used for pathogen recognition, as shown in Fig. 5 A, vancomycin-functionalized gold nanoparticles (VAN-Au NPs) were firstly prepared, due to the D-Ala-D-Ala-termini of VAN can bind with S. aureus via the hydrogen bonding effect. Besides, IgG was pre-coated on the T-line to capture S. aureus through the interaction between Fc region of antibody and protein A on the S. aureus [86]. By this means, the VAN-Au NPs-S. aureus-IgG sandwich-type complex was formed. To further improve the sensitivity of the assay, the in-situ gold growth procedure was applied to increase the size and color of AuNPs, the LOD of S. aureus is 103 CFU/mL.

The SERS method was also used to improve the sensitivity of the detection. A SERS aptasensor was developed for S. aureus detection in fish [48], aptamers of S. aureus were firstly linked with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) coated on polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) film (served as capture substrate), then gold-silver core-shell nanoflowers (Au@Ag NFs) were synthesized and modified with 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MBA) and aptamers as the signal probe. In the presence of S. aureus, a “capture substrate-target-signal probe” sandwich structure was formed, the SERS signal of MBA at 1085 cm−1 was linearly related to the S. aureus concentration, and the LOD of S. aureus is 13 CFU/mL, which was much sensitive than conventional methods. However, the high concentrations of salt ions should be carefully removed before detection to keep the biosensors stable.

Salmonella bacteria are frequently found in aquatic food products and induce severe symptoms in the individual, like diarrhea, fever, and abdominal cramps after ingesting the contaminated food, which can be fatal in some severe cases [90,91]. LFA coupled with different SERS probes was used for multi-targets detection in salmon, oysters, and shrimp [87], as shown in Fig. 5B, PDSM film was coated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and functionalized with two specific aptamers, besides, aptamer modified AuNPs integrated with Raman reporter (4-MBA/Nile blue A (NBA)) were prepared as SERS probes, the sandwich structure PDSM-Au-pathogen-SERS probes complex were formed in the presence of pathogens, the enhanced SERS signals were recorded using portable SERS instrument, as low as 18 CFU/mL and 27 CFU/mL V. parahaemolyticus and S. typhimurium can be detected, respectively.

To simplify the operation procedure and improve the detection efficiency, A single-tube assay has been developed for the visual detection of Salmonella in oysters and fish without tedious operations [88]. As shown in Fig. 5C, the in-situ DNA extraction, amplification, and visualization were integrated into one tube. Positively charged chitosan was used for diatomaceous earth (DE) functionalization, and the complex was used for DNA extraction through electrostatic interaction between chitosan and DNA (fimY gene) in Salmonella, the self-settling property of DE enables centrifuge-free extraction of DNA within 3 min, after 14 min DNA sequence amplification by PCR, the amounts of DNA was visualized by the naked eyes under UV light, the LOD of the assay is 1 CFU/mL.

L. monocytogenes is the causative agent of listeriosis that can lead to important illness scenarios such as meningitis, fetal anomalies, abortion, febrile gastroenteritis, or even generalized infection [92,93]. Meanwhile, L. monocytogenes can survive in a wide variety of environments, like as high as 20 % (v/v) NaCl concentration, a wide range of temperatures (0.5–45 °C), and pH values (4.0–9.5) [94,95]. It is vital to sensitively detect the L. monocytogenes in aquatic products.

Multicomponent nanomaterials often have superior properties, like highly active surface and interface, being able to conjugate different functional molecules, dielectric confinement effect, and enhanced catalytic ability [96,97]. A chemiluminescence (CL) assay for L. monocytogenes detection in canned tuna and oyster had been developed by using hybrid materials with catalytic properties, as shown in Fig. 5D, both of the penicillin-modified magnetic beads and Cu/Co/Ni TNA Cu/Co/Ni ternary nanocatalyst (Cu/Co/Ni TNA) functionalized antibody was able to attach to the surface of L. monocytogenes and formed a specific sandwich complex. Due to Cu/Co/Ni TNA can greatly enhance the CL signal of the hydroxylamine-O-sulfonic acid (HOSA)-luminol system. After magnetic separation, L. monocytogenes were detected by recording CL signals, the LOD of this assay is 70 CFU/mL.

In conclusion, by changing different recognition molecules (i.e., antibodies, aptamers, antibiotics, lectins, borate groups et al.) on biosensors, different types of pathogenic microorganisms can be quantitatively determined by LFA, spectroscopy, and electrochemical methods. However, the cross-reactivity between different pathogens with biosensors, the interferences of matrix components on the stability of nanoprobes, as well as the cost of nanotechnology-based detection compared with the conventional methods need to be specially addressed in the practical test. These problems can be alleviated by seeking recognition molecules with high affinity, magnetic-assisted sample pretreatment, and the development of miniaturized portable instruments. In addition, the development of high-throughput detection methods is also important for food safety control in the future due to the presence of multiple pathogens in aquatic products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications for harmful metal ions detection
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their potential hazards to public health, heavy metal ions (e.g., Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+) in aquatic products have been of increasing concern [98]. Since the enrichment effect through the food chain, long-term intake of these heavy metal ions, even at a low level, will cause some serious consequences, like acute and chronic diseases, such as cognitive deficits, kidney failure, cardiovascular, and neurological disorders [99]. In addition, since the illegal discharge of chemical waste into the inland water and ocean, Cu2+ also easily exceeded the safety standard for human health. Many instrumental analysis methods, such as atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry, and mass spectrometry were used for heavy metal ions detection [98]. Nevertheless, these techniques still have certain deficiencies, like cost and time-consuming, and often need specialized operators [100]. Currently, nanotechnology-based analytical methods are widely used in the detection of toxic heavy metal ions (see Table 2 ). Many of them are rapid, simple, selective, and sensitive, which play an important role in the detection of metal ions in aquatic products [101–103].

Mercuric (Hg2+) ions released from industrial sources are regarded as the global pollutant of the world's environment, which can contaminate the soil and water, being enriched in the aquatic organisms and entering people's bodies through the food chain [114], Hg2+ has a neurotoxic effect on human health even at low concentrations. Rapid and cost-effective methods are urgently required for the detection of Hg2+ in aquatic products [115,116].

The interaction between Hg2+ and the amino or carboxyl groups in CDs was applied for Hg2+ detection, as shown in Fig. 6 A, a signal “turn-off” fluorescent assay was developed for Hg2+ detection in fish samples (rainbow trout) [117]. CDs were embedded in the ferrisilicates zeolite (NaFZ) by hydrothermal/solvothermal method. The composite material has improved stability, high emission intensity, and a high quantum yield percentage of 91.2 %. The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased after Hg2+ binding onto the complex. A low detection limit of 5.5 pM was achieved with a wide detection range of 0.1–1500 nM.

Lead ion (Pb2+) is a longstanding environmental contaminant that is harmful to human health, such as kidney disease and reduction in hemoglobin formation, mental retardation, infertility, and abnormalities in human beings [120,121]. High concentrations of Pb2+ were found in industrial waste, such as the effluents from the production of batteries, alloys, gasoline additives, and pigments, and the indiscriminate discharge of wastewater resulted in the accumulation of Pb2+ in the food chain [122], especially in aquatic products.

Similar to the interaction of Hg2+ and T-rich nucleic acid, Pb2+ can also induce the conformation change of G-rich single-strand DNA, this phenomenon is employed for the design of Pb2+ probe, as shown in Fig. 6B, an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor contained MOFs and QDs had been developed for multiple determination of Hg2+ and Pb2+ since the MOFs have large surface areas and large pore volumes, QDs possess high quantum yield, tunable emission, and good electrical conductivity [118], G-rich aptamers labeled with PtNPs and T-rich aptamers labeled with AuNPs were used to recognize Pb2+ and Hg2+, respectively. Pb2+ can induce the formation of a G-quadruplex and result in the nanoparticles detaching from the electrodes, therefore, the ECL signal was decreased significantly. Pb2+ and Hg2+ can be detected in fish and shrimp, with the LOD of Hg2+ and Pb2+ being 4.1 × 10−12 M and 2.4 × 10−11 M, respectively.

Pb2+ selective DNAzyme is widely used for constructing Pb2+ probes through Pb2+ induced DNA cleavage, as shown in Fig. 6C, a label-free DNAzyme assay was used for Pb2+ detection in fish [109]. Pb2+ can trigger the DNAzyme cleavage, leading to the exposure of the 3′-terminal of both substrate and DNAzyme, which were further digested to DNA fragments by the Exo I; on the contrary, the digestion process was blocked in the absence of Pb2+, the intact double strands DNAzyme can bind with nucleic acid dyes (e.g., SYBR Green I) and produce an enhanced fluorescent signal, as low as 0.12 nM Pb2+ can be detected.

Cadmium ion (Cd2+) is a toxic heavy metal ion that originated from widespread sources including industrial wastes, fossil fuel consumption, and the mining industry. Cd2+ tends to accumulate in the kidney. Long-term exposure to Cd2+ leads to metabolic changes and damage to kidney tubular cells [123,124]. It is vital for Cd2+ detection in aquatic food.

As shown in Fig. 6D, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor had been prepared for Cu2+ and Cd2+ detection in scallops [119]. The nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) can attach to reduced glutathione (GSH) functionalized gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) through electrostatic interactions. The fluorescence intensity of GSH-AuNCs at 565 nm can be quenched by Cu2+ and enhanced by Cd2+ while the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs at 403 nm stayed constant (served as the internal standard). By this means, Cu2+ and Cd2+ can be detected with the LOD as 4.12 nM and 943 nM, respectively.

Due to the specific inhibition properties of heavy metal ions on the activity of enzymes, the metal ions can be detected indirectly by measuring the catalytical ability of enzymes. A portable microfluidic device had been developed for heavy metal ions (Hg2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+) detection in fish gill and muscle tissue [125], urease functionalized magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles (MNPs) were used as probes, the activity of urease was inhibited after the –SH group in the urease binds with the heavy metal ions, the hydrophobic microchannels help in settling the MNPs in the confined area, which produced a more clear chromogenic color change compared with the microwell based assay, the LOD of these ions are 0.1 ng/L in this assay.

Copper (Cu2+) is an essential trace element in the human body, which plays an important role in many physiological balances of the human body. However, excessive intake of Cu2+ will lead to oxidative stress in the liver, irreversible hepatotoxicity, and disturb intracellular homeostasis [119]. It is necessary to develop a rapid and accurate technique for the detection of Cu2+ in aquatic products [126].

Molecules with functional groups (e.g., –SH, –COOH, –NH2) can coordinate with Cu2+ and are used for the development of Cu2+ sensors. Fluorescent CdSe QDs functionalized with 3-mercaptopropionic acid (3-MPA) were used for Cu2+ detection in oysters [126], the mercapto group (-SH) and carboxyl group (-COOH) of 3-MPA are easily coordinated with Cu2+ and affect the recombination efficiency of electrons and holes in the CdSe QDs, therefore, the fluorescence intensity of QDs was quenched. The LOD of Cu2+ is 0.03 μM. Fluorescent polydopamine-polyethyleneimine (PDA-PEI) copolymer dots were also used for Cu2+ detection in the oysters [127], as shown in Fig. 7 A, the coordination of amino groups and Cu2+ induced the quenching of fluorescence of PDA-PEI copolymer dots, the LOD of Cu2+ is 1.6 nM.

Compared with the above-mentioned metal ions which have specific aptamers, the biosensors reported so far for Cu2+ detection are mainly based on the coordination reaction. Therefore, the selectivity and anti-interference ability of these methods still need to be improved.

In addition to the above-mentioned metal ions, several other metal ions found in aquatic products also have serious impacts on the ecological environment and human health due to their toxicity, persistence, and non-biodegradable properties, like As (III/V), Ni2+, and Cr6+ et al. [130].

High concentrations of inorganic As ions are harmful to human health, e.g., skin damage, circulatory system issues, protein coagulation, nerve inflammation, muscle weakness, and carcinogenicity [131]. Seafood and fish are major contributors to arsenic in the diet [132,133]. A phosphorescence chemosensor had been developed for quantitatively determining inorganic arsenic (As(III) plus As(V)) in fish samples [128], as shown in Fig. 7B, the probe was prepared by coating Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots with (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) and an As ionic imprinted polymer, the LOD for total inorganic As was 29.6 μg/kg, which was lower than the EU/EC regulation limits.

Many studies have proved that long-term exposure to Ni2+ can cause skin allergies, lung fibrosis, cardiovascular disease, respiratory tract cancer, and asthma [134]. A magnetic nano-adsorbent silk fibroin-EDTA ligand (SF–Fe3O4-EDTA) was designed for Ni2+ detection in crabs and oysters [135]. The magnetic Fe3O4 was formed in situ on the fibroin which has a high surface area and possesses lots of functional groups, while EDTA on the fibroin can coordinate with Ni2+ in the solutions, the LOD of Ni2+ is 1.7 ng/L by using furnace atomic absorption method.

Cr6+ is highly toxic and carcinogenic to biochemical systems and long-term exposure to Cr6+ will cause bronchitis, liver, kidney, and nerve tissue damage, and even death in a large dose. As shown in Fig. 7C, a colorimetric assay was developed for Cr6+ detection in fish (tuna) by using a single-atom nanozyme [129], SA-Fe/NG peroxidase mimetic was prepared by anchoring Fe single-atom (SA-Fe) onto 2D nitrogen-doped graphene (NG), which was able to oxidize the TMB and formed blue color product (oxTMB), this process was inhibited by 8-hydroxyquinoline (8-HQ) due to the electrostatic interactions. Due to Cr6+ can specifically interact with 8-HQ, the catalytic property of SA-Fe/NG was recovered. By this means, Cr6+ can be detected in the linear range of 30 nM to 3 μM with a LOD of 3 nM.

In conclusion, many nanotechnologies have been developed for metal ions detection through colorimetric, fluorescent, and electrochemical methods, which are simple, sensitive, time and cost-efficient. However, there is still room for improvement. 1) Many CDs-based fluorescent methods are mainly based on the covalent interaction between metal ions and functional groups on the surface (e.g., amino and carboxyl groups), the exact reaction process needs to be studied. 2) The accuracy of detection is easily disturbed by coexisting metal ions and matrix compounds, which need recognition probes with higher specificity and multifunctional materials to improve the selectivity of detection. 3) Electrochemical methods using different electrolytic voltages to detect each metal ion, as well as nanomaterials modified with different recognition molecules and signaling molecules are promising and deserve more attention from researchers.

Marine toxins, like tetrodotoxin (TTX) [136], shellfish toxins (e.g., okadaic acid, OA) [137,138], and microcystin-LR (MC-LR) [139–141] are highly toxic, and can severely affect human health, wildlife, and the ecosystem [140,142,143]. ELISA and HPLC-based methods have been widely used for biotoxin detection [144–146], which usually needs special antibodies or tedious instrument operations. Therefore, nanomaterials and signal amplification strategies are adopted for the detection of these marine toxins (see Table 3 ).

OA is a shellfish toxin that can cause diarrheic disorder by inhibiting serine/threonine protein phosphatase. Multiple DNA amplification processes are applied to enhance the sensitivity of OA detection, as shown in Fig. 8 A, aptamer-functionalized copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) were used for fluorescent detection of OA in shellfish [156]. A duplexed aptamer was immobilized on the magnetic beads, in the presence of OA, the duplex aptamer melted and produced a single-strand DNA, which acts as a template to produce poly (thymine, T) tails by using terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT)-mediated isothermal amplification system, the poly(T) segment further act as a template for the in-situ formation of fluorescent CuNPs. Therefore, OA can be detected in the linear detection range of 1–50 ng/L, and the LOD of OA is 1.1 ng/L.

TTX is frequently found in the blood, liver, and ovary of puffer, and has acute and serious toxicity (often lethal) to humans. A fluorescence assay was established for TTX detection in clams and shellfish, which relies on aptamer recognition and triple isothermal amplification (i.e., strand displacement amplification (SDA) combined with catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA)) [157], as shown in Fig. 8B, free TTX and complementary DNA (cDNA) compete for binding to aptamer modified magnetic bead, the unbound cDNA was used as a primer for the DNA amplification, this triple cycle amplification (SDA-CHA-SDA) has a much better performance than conventional single amplification method, and the sensitivity is 3 times higher than the ELISA kit with a LOD of 0.265 pg/mL.

The multimodal method can enhance the accuracy and versatility of detection, as shown in Fig. 8C, a dual-modal fluorescence and SERS aptasensor had been developed for TTX detection in puffer fish and clam samples [158], gold nanoparticles embedded MOFs hybrids (AuNPs@MIL-101) were used as SERS substrates, then fluorescence dye (Cy3) labeled aptamer were adsorbed onto the AuNPs@MIL-101 which served as the recognition element and signal probe (Cy3-aptamer). The binding of TTX with aptamer induced the release of Cy3-aptamer-tetrodotoxin complex from AuNPs@MIL-101, which enhanced the fluorescence signal intensity and decreased SERS signals. The LOD of TTX by using fluorescence and SERS methods are 6 and 8 pg/mL, respectively.

MC-LR is a kind of cyclic hepatotoxic heptapeptide produced by several species of cyanobacteria, including microcystis aeruginosa, which has significant detrimental effects (skin irritation, diarrhea, damage to the liver, and carcinogenesis) on humans, and can accumulate in aquatic products like fish, shellfish, and prawns. Thus, it is important and urgent to develop fast and sensitive analytical methods for the quantitation of MC-LR [160].

A nanozyme-based colorimetric method had been established for MC-LR detection [159], as shown in Fig. 8D, core-shell silica/nickel silicate (SiO2@Ni silicate) was coated onto the microplate, and acted as the substrate to capture MC-LR. The MC-LR was specifically recognized by the antibody (Ab1), then secondary antibody (Ab2) modified Cu(OH)2 nanozyme served as a substrate for loading DNAzymes (G-quadruplex/hemin). MC-LR can be visually detected by the multi-enzymes assist signal amplification in a concentration range from 0.007 to 75 μg/mL with a LOD of 6 ng/mL.

Microfluidic chip biosensors also play an important role in marine toxin detection since they are compact, reagent-saving, and customizable. A magnet-actuated droplet microfluidic competitive immunosensor had been developed for MC-LR detection in fish and prawn [160], 30 μL mixture of antibody-enzyme (HRP-Ab) and MC-LR complex, 30 μL washing buffer, and 30 μL fluorescent substrate were added in three sequential tandem wells, respectively. The antigen-conjugated magnetic bead (Mb-Ag) was added in the first droplet and reacted with HRP-Ab, then washed in the second well, and reacted with the substrate in the third well. Finally, the produced fluorescence signal was recorded with a gel imager. The LOD of MC-LR is 0.6 μg/mL with high specificity. There are three advantages of this assay: 1) simple but effective operation by magnetic force rather than inconvenient actuating devices, 2) high-throughput detection ability on a 15-channel chip, and 3) the convenient signal readout on an ordinary gel imager as the signal reader.

In conclusion, nanotechnology has been successfully used for marine toxins detection, however, it still needs improvement in practical application, e.g., the aptamer sequence is relatively long (often more than 50 bases), which increases the design difficulty of probes; the affinity of biosensors to target molecules is not strong. It is promising to use truncated aptamer without sacrificing the binding affinity and find molecules with higher reaction activity and selectivity.

Biogenic amines (BA) are frequently found in many contaminated or spoiled aquatic products and are produced by several microorganisms (bacteria, yeasts, and molds) during the decarboxylation process of amino acids by enzymatic reactions. The main BAs found in aquatic products are putrescine, tyramine, histamine, spermine, and spermidine et al. [161]. High concentrations of BAs have toxic effects due to their psychoactive, vasoactive, and hypertensive properties. The symptoms include respiratory difficulties, nausea, vomiting, palpitation, perspiration, hypo- or hypertension gastric and intestinal problems, urticarial, and headache. It is important to simply, quickly, accurately, and sensitively detect BAs in aquatic products, therefore, several nanotechnologies were developed for BAs detection (see Table 4 ) [162].

Trimethylamine (TMA) is produced during the spoilage of seafood, which is usually employed as the symbol material to determine the freshness of fish and shrimp. A hydrogel loaded with silver-doped Prussian blue nanoparticles (SPB NPs) was used for colorimetric and photothermal monitoring of the concentration of TMA in bighead carp and greasyback shrimp [167], TMA can decompose SPB NPs after penetrating the hydrogel and formed blue color product, by this means, TMA can be detected from 0.21 to 0.54 ppm by measuring colorimetric and photothermal signals.

The electrochemistry method using enzyme mimic species with high electroactivity also plays an important role in the detection of biogenic amine. An electrochemical method was proposed to detect histamine in river crabs [168], as shown in Fig. 9 A, the aptamer of histamine (Apt) was conjugated onto a gold nanoflower (AuNFs) modified ITO electrode (Apt/AuNFs/ITO), and cDNA with peroxidase mimetic activity was linked onto the Au@FeCo nanohybrids. Since the histamine competitively hindered the hybridization of aptamer and cDNA, the amounts of Au@FeCo nanohybrid on the electrode were decreased, and the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) signal was also signal-off, the LOD of histamine is 0.79 nM with a wide detection range as 1–1000 nM.

SERS is a sensitive technique that can significantly amplify the detection signal. To eliminate the interference of coexisting components of similar structures in the spoiled aquatic products on the detection, thin-layer-chromatography (TLC) acts as the simple platform to separate complex samples and concentrate the target, as shown in Fig. 9B, TLC coupled with SERS (TLC-SERS) had been developed for on-site separation and detection of histamine in fish [169]. The histamine was first separated by TLC on a diatomite plate and then derivatized with fluorescamine (FSA). Then, 2 μL gold nanoparticles were deposited onto the analyte spot for SERS detection, the LOD of histamine is 9 ppb.

Since many biogenic amines are produced simultaneously during the seafood deterioration process, it is important to develop high-throughput and multi-target sensing protocols. As shown in Fig. 9C, an AuNPs-based colorimetric chemosensor array was developed for the quantitative detection of multiple biogenic amines (e.g., histamine, spermine, putrescine et al.) in fish [170], three carboxylate derivatives were decorated on the AuNPs, which capture target amines through hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions, therefore, 10 amine derivatives were simultaneously discriminated by a linear discriminant analysis (LDA) based on the multi-colorimetric response pattern of structural diﬀerences, the LOD of histamine is 7.2 ppm.

In summary, we have discussed the recently developed nanotechnologies that have been widely adopted in constructing biosensors, as well as for pretreating seafood samples for food safety and quality evaluation. These methods have attractive advantages: being user-friendly, and easily accessible. Many nanomaterials possess superior properties, like good conductivity, sensitive colorimetric or fluorescence response to the surrounding environments, and high specific surface areas for conjugating functional molecules. The applications of these methods were demonstrated by detecting contaminants in fish, shrimp, crabs et al., including foodborne pathogens, harmful metal ions, marine biotoxins, and biogenic amines. Benefiting from the characteristics of various nanomaterials, like catalytic, optical, magnetic, and electronic properties, harmful species in food were quantitatively detected with high accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity. However, there are still improvements in these nanotechnologies.
(1)
Although nanomaterial-based sensors have shown the potential in sensing aspects of food areas, as aquatic food samples are complex in composition, the interference of matrix, as well as the cross interference of different species are still serious problems [171]. Thus, more strategies should be used to improve the specificity of the detection, like efficient pretreatment of samples to separate the targets of interest, signal amplification, as well as improving the binding affinity of recognition molecules. The magnetic assist separation approach is a promising strategy due to its high binding ability, ease of conduct, and the ability to use multiple cycles.
(2)
Although nanoprobes are widely used in a series of detection methods, their good performance is still limited by many environmental factors, like high ion concentration, reaction time, temperature, and pH value of the reaction system et al. The size, morphology, and surface groups also play an important role in nanoprobe activities and functions. It is vital to control the synthesis and functionalization procedures of nanoprobes since they can cause the inconsistency of each batch of nanoprobes and affect the application of nanoprobes in practical sample detection. Therefore, it is meaningful to study the surface and interface chemistry during the formation of nanomaterials, to controllable prepare functional nanomaterials with long-term stability and good reproducibility.
(3)
Considering the high possibility of multiple contaminants co-exist in aquatic food, as well as the requirement of fast, simple, and in-situ food safety measurement, it is valuable to prepare nanoprobes with multifunction, like simultaneously sensing, target capturing, and harmful analytes removing in food. Meanwhile, it is important to design small equipment that integrates sample pretreatment, target detection, signal amplification, and read-out functions. Furthermore, the high-throughput analysis and multi-target detection capability of biosensors need to be improved to reduce the cost and time of operation. It is promising to prepare nanoprobes with multi-functional recognition units and develop microfluid channels with different labels.
(4)
Although a series of detection approaches were designed for aquatic food safety detection, it is important to understand some basic processes. Questions regarding several aspects of nano sensing need to be addressed, including the signal amplification property of nanozyme, synergetic catalytical enhancement of multi-enzymes functionalized materials, the kinetics of interactions, and the recognition process of targets in a heterogeneous surface and interface of nanoprobes.
(5)
Although nanotechnologies for a series of contaminants detection were discussed, many other emerging contaminants were also important, like plastic residues, degradation-resistant antibiotics and pesticides, and radioactive material (131I, 90Sr, 137Cs et al.) that discharged into the ocean, these species can move into the bodies of marine fish all over the world, which may lead to serious consequences due to the very slow degradation rate. It is vital to develop sensitive detection methods and effective management strategies.
